subject: Agricultural Science
q: List and discuss briefly eight factors that militate against the developement of commercial agriculture in Nigeria.
explanation:
Eight factors that militate against the development of agricukature in Nigeria are; (i) Inadequate Land (ii) Poor storaage and processing facilities (iii) Inadequate finance (iv) Inadequate transportation network (v) Lack of farm inputs (vi) Lack of basic amenities (vii) inadequate agricultural education and extension services (viii) poor marketing system.

 (1) Inadequate Land (i) increase in population reduces the size of farmlands. (ii) The type of rand tenure systems Nigeria prevents farmers from acquiring land, (iii) Deforestation contributes to lack of land

(2) Poor storage and processing facilities: (i) Excess produce are wasted due to lack of storage facilities (iii) Storage faciities are expensive to purchase. (iii) improper storage and processing reduce the quantity and quality of the produce.

(3) Inadequate finance: (i) Peasant farmers have no access to loan or credit facilities, (iii) High interest rate charge by banks discourage borrowing. (di) Poor farmers who manage to get loans divert them to other purposes

(4) Inadequate transportation: (i) most farms in Nigeria are not accessible by roads. (ii) Lack of sufficient vehicle  hinders movement of food from rural to urban areas, (iii) High cost of transportation is a problem to commercial farming" (Iv) Some of the existing roads in rural areas are in very bad condition.

(5) Lack of farm input: (i) Farm inputs like tools, fertilizers are inadequate. (ii) Farm inputs are of inferior quality. (iii) Farm inputs even wh-en available are not supplied to farmers at the right time,

(6) Lack of basic amenities; Basic amenities like, electricity, pipe borne water and hospitals are tacking in rural areas

(7) Inadequate agricultural education and extension services: (i) Majority of the farmers cannot read and 'write (ii) They do not know how to apply modern farm inputs (iii) Extension officers are inadequate.

(8) Poor marketing system: (i) There are no organised marketing channels for produce. (ii) There is lack of propel ,pricing for agrlc produce. (iii) The activities of middlemen worsen the marketing of produce.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) What does mechaniztion mean in Agriculture?

(b) What are the advantages and disadvantages of mechanization?

(c) State and discuss briefly four limitations of mechanization in Agriculture?
explanation:
Mechanization in Agriclture simply means the use of modern farm inputs to increase productivity and efficiency. It also involves the use of motorised equipments like tractors, planters, farm implements like plough, harrow, ridgers etc and also the use of chemicals, improved seeds and fertilizers to increase production.

Advantages of mechanization are (i) Mechanization increases production (ii) It reduces health hazards (iii) it supplements human power for heavy duty job (iv) There is timeliness in farm operation. (v) It encourages large scale farming (vi) It makes naturally difficult and unpleasent manual jobs, easy and pleasant

Disadvantages of mechanization are (i) Displacement of workers (ii) Destruction of soil structure (iii) It causes environmental pollution (iv) There is compaction of soil by machines

Limitations of agricultural mechanization are (i) Economic Limitation: farmers are generally poor. They do not have money to buy inputs like tractors, fertilizers, chemicals, seeds, etc (ii) Technical know-how: Most farmers do not know how to operate machines or apply fertilizers. They do not read instructions attached to the implements. (iii) small holdings of of farmlands: Most farmers cultivate small farmlands because they are poor and as a result of thetype of land tenure system practiced in Nigeria (iv) Lack of maintenance: Farmers cannot repair farm tools and implements. Spare parts are scarce, expensive with no trained personnel to repair machines.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Write short notes on the effects of the following farm practices on soil: (a) bush burning, (b) fertlizer application (c) grazing (d) crop rotation
explanation:
Bush Burning: (i) It destroys the seeds of weeds, (ii) It destroys soil borne pathogene (iii) it burns the organic matter content of soil (iv) it kills or reduces the number of soil organisms. (v) It exposes the soil to erosion

Fertlizer application: (i) It supplements nutrient content of the soil (ii) it promotes productive capacity of the soil (iii) it increases the population of organisms in the soil (iv) Excessive use of fertilizer can cause soil acidity

Over grazing: (i) It depletes the vegetation of the soil (ii) It exposes the soil to erosion (iii) It destroys the soil structure (iv) It leads to the compaction of the soil

Crop rotation: (i) It increases the yield of crops (ii) It controls erosion and weed growth (iii) It controls pests and diseases (iv) It ensures the fertility of the soil

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) State six major uses of land in Nigeria (b) Discuss briefly five factors affecting land availablility for agricultural production
explanation:
(i) For production of crops, (ii) Land is the natural grazing area for lives-tock (iii) Land is used for Wild life and game reserves. (iv)  Land Is used for forestry. (v) Land can also be used for residential and industrial buildings. (vi) Land is used for construction of roads, railway lines etc 

Five factors affecting Availability of land for agricultural production include: (i) Increase in population; Increase in population of the people reduces he availability of land for farming (ii) Land tenure system: The type of land tenure system in Nigeria does not make land available for farming. (iii) Topography: The shape of the land affects its availability. If the land is sloppy, swampy, hilly etc, such land may not be available for farming (iv) Cultural practices: Cultural practices like bush burning and shifting cultivation reduce the availability of land for farming. (v) Soil type: This affects the availability but if the soil is loamy such land will be available for farming

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) List ten common grass and legume species of Nigerian pastures giving their common and botanical names.

(b) Discuss three factors affecting the distribution of pastures in Nigeria
explanation:
Common and botanical names of Five grass specie are:  

Elephant grass  - Pennisetum purpurem

Guinea grass - Panicum maximum

Giant star grass - Cynodon plectostahyum

Carpet grass  - Axonopus compressus

Spear grass - Imperata cylindrical

Common and botanical names of Five legume specie are:  

Centro - Centrosema pubescens

Stylo - Stylosanthes gracilic

Kudzu or puero - Pueraria phaseoloideslia

Calopo - Calopogonium mucunoides

Cowpea - Vigna ungiculata

Three factors affecting the distribution of pasture in Nigeria are (i) Rainfall: The amount of rainfail determines the nature of pasture. Too much or no rainfall prevent the growth of pasture. (ii) Soil type; type of soil determines the rate of growth of pasture. Some soils  to favour pasture growth than others, (iii) Pests and diseases: High incidence of pests and diseases in an area tends to reduce the quality and quantity of pasture

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss briefly a named oil crop under the following headings (a) climatic and soil requirements (b) planting date, seed rate and spacing (c) nursery practices (d) harvesting (e) processing and storage
explanation:
A named oil crop is oil palm (a) Climatic and soil requirement of oil palm include; Temperature requirement is 18°C while rainfall Is 150 - 250crn per annum. It requires a deep loamy soil rich in humus with a pH of 4.5 - 6.0

(b) Planting date: Pre-nursery is August - October. Nursery is nine months later while field planting is March - May a year later . Spacing: (i) Pre-nursery is 7.6cm x 7.6cm (ii) Nursery is 60cm x 60cm while (iii) Field is 7.8 x 9m

(c) Nursery practices; (i) Pre-nursery: Seeds go through certain processes of germination. Germination occurs 15 days later (ii) Nursery: Raised seed trays are used and seedlings are raised till four months later within which weeding, supplying, watering etc are done

(d) Harvesting: Oil palm matures after 3-5 years. Harvesting knives are used to remove mature bunch from the tree.

(e) Processing and storage: Fruits are boiled, Pounded and the seeds and fibres are removed. The thick liquid is later boiled and the oil is removed from the liquid. Oil palm is stored in containers made of  Copper, zinc or aluminium

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Write short notes on four nutrient livesock under the following headings (a) composition (b) sources (c) functions
explanation:
Short notes on four food nutrients of livestocks are: (i) Carbohydrates: Cornposition: it contains carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. Sources: From maize, guinea corn, cassava. Functions: It provides energy to the animals

(ii) Proteins: composition: It contains nitrogen, carbon, hydrogen, oxygen. phopphorus and sulphur. Sources: Fish meal. groundnut Ike, soyabeans meals, legumes Function:: Helps in the replacement of worn-out tissues, for growth. production of eggs and meat.

(iii) Fats & Oil: Composition: .it contains carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. Sources: Oil seeds like groundnut Cake and palm kernel cake. Functions: It supplies energy and conserve body heat.

(iv) Minerals: composition: Sodium, Calcium, phosphorous, Magnesium etc. Sources: Bone meal, oystershell, limestone, synthetic minerals, salt licks etc. Functions: Bone, teeth, eggshell formation and development, constituent of blood, acid-base balance.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) Describe the process of egg-formation in poultry

(b) Enumerate six functions/roles of the reproductive hormones in animals
explanation:
The process of egg formation in poultry is controlled by hormones. The egg is formed partly in the ovary and partly the oviduct. Yolk is formed In the ovary while other proteinous parts are formed in the liver and kidney. The yolk released by the ovary is taken up by infidibuIum and later moved to the oviduct where fertilization takes place The egg moves to the magnum, stay there for three hours and part of the albumen is formed. Chalaza is secreted round the yolk from the magnum. the egg moves to the isthmus where it stays for 1¼ hours and the two shell membrane, are formed. The shape of the egg is also formed in this region. The egg then, moves to the uterus where it slays for 19-20 hours, The Shell is formed in the uterus. The egg is then passed to the vagina where it stays for a very short time before it is laid

Six functions/roles of the reproductive hormones in animals are: (i) Androgen stimulates the development of  male secondary sexual characters and sex behaviours. (ii) It stimulates the optimum condition for spern production (iii) estrogen helps in the production of heat in fernales. (iv) It also regulates menstrual cycle and ovulation (v) progesterone helps in the continuance of pregnancy (vi) Oxytocin helps in causing milk production.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) State and discuss briefly four factors of production in Agriculture

(b) List four functions of a farm manager.
explanation:
(a) Four factors of production in agriculture are:

(1) Land:  (i) Land refers to where production can take place. (ii) It s the gift of nature (iii) It Is immobile and deteriorates when it ts not properly used. (v) It also refers to soil,water.minerals (v) The reward for land is rent,

(2) Labour: (I) It refers to human effort put into production (ii) it includes personal, family, communal and hired or paid labour. (iii) Labour can be skilled, unkilled or semi-skilled. (iv) The reward for labour is wages or Salary.

(3) Capital; (i) This includes all man-made productive assets which are used in production. (i) It could be fixed, e.g. buildings, vehicles, tools and implements. (iii) It could be working capital e.g. water, feed, drug et.c (iv) The reward for capital is interest.

(4) Entrepreneur or Management: (i) This refers to the person who coordinates, organises and controls the use of other factors of production, (iii) In agriculture, he is referred to as the Farm Manager.

(b)Four functions of a Farm Manager are: (i) Organisation (ii) Administration (iii) Production (iv) Marketing

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Write short notes on the following as they relate to the marketing of agricultural produce (a) Marketing/commodity Boards (b)  Co-operative Societies (c) Middlemen/Wholesalers (d) Producers
explanation:
Marketing/Commodity Boards as they relate to the marketing or agricultural produce are: (i)These are public bodies set up by the government to handle the sales of agricultural products. (ii) They stabilize the prices of produce (iii) They ensure sales promotion of export crops (iv) They render export services to farmers (v) They collect and transport crops.

(b) Co-operative Societies (i) They are trading organisations where the cooperators (members) pool their resources together with a view to making profit (ii) They stimulate competition in produce marketing (iii) They provide storage facilities (iv) They also provide transportation services (v) They can grant loans to members

(c) Middlemen/Wholesalers: (i) They buy goods in large quanitities and sell in small quantities to retailers (ii) They provide storage facilities (iii) They have good transport facilities (iv) They pass information from retailers to producers.

(d) Producers: Theysell directly to consumers (ii) Consumers get goods at reduced prices (iii) Produce get to the consumers fresh.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
Study the farm tools illustrated by diagrams I -VI below and use them to answer Questions 1(a) - (c).

(a) Name the tools illustrated by diagram I -VI

(b) State one use of each of the tools illustrated by diagrams I -VI.

(c) State one method of maintenance for each of the tools illustrated by diagrams I -VI.
explanation:
(a) Farm tools;

  • I = Knapsacks sprayer
  • Il = Watering can
  • Ill = Sieve
  • IV = Sickle (short handle)
  • V = Spanner
  • VI = Feeder/ Waterer

(b) Uses;

  • I - Spraying chemicals such as pesticides, insecticides, herbicides and liquid fertilizers.
  • Il - application of water to crops (during the season), application of liquid fertilizers.
  • IlI - used for separation of soil particles., to sieve compost of F.Y.M. to make nursery soil., to analyse the texture of the soil., for seed separation in seed processing
  • IV - used for harvesting fruits/cereal crops., used for cutting grass pasture for animals
  • V -used for loosening and tightening of nuts on farm machinery.
  • VI - used for feeding chicks/fowls in poultry farm., used also as drinker to provide water for chicks/fowls.

(c) Methods of maintenance;

  • I -should be properly rinsed with water after use to get rid of left-over chemicals. Open up clogged nozzle or clean the nozzle and keep it in a cool, dry place.
  • Il - should be drained after use, stored upside down; rinse /wash with water if used for liquid fertilizers; keep in a cool dry place.
  • Ill - store in a cool, dry place and clean after use
  • IV -sharpen regularly the blunt edges; apply grease to avoid rusting and clean after use and store in an area free regularly. from rain and termites. 
  • V - store in a cool dry place and clean after use.
  • VI - store in a cool, dry place and clean

(a) Name the end-product obtained.

(ii) How can the deficiency symptoms be corrected?
explanation:
.(a) The end product of compost making is organic manure or compost

(b) Advantages of Organic manure:

  • It promotes the activities of living creatures or microorganisms in the soil.
  • It adds more humus to the soil, thus increasing the nutrients for plants growth. 
  • It improves soil structure by binding loose particles together.
  • absorbs heat readily during the day and loses it slowly during the night due to its dark colour.  
  • It controls soil temperature.
  • It helps to conserve soil water.
  • It balances the acid/base condition of the soil.
  • it prevents erosion by improving the structure and reducing run-off.
  • It aids aeration of the soil.

(c)(i) Vertical section through a virgin land

(ii) The diagram is a soil profile

(d)(i) The crops are deficient in nitrogen,
(ii) Deficiency symptoms are corrected by the application of nitrogenous fertilizers, e.g. NPK, Urea, (NH\(_{4}\)), SO\(_{4}\), Ammonium nitrate.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
A farmer applied one-third of the required amount of N.P.K fertilizer to 2 hectares of maize at the rate of 50 kg N.P.K, fertilizer per hectare, two weeks after planting.

Use the information above to answer questions (a) to (d) below:

(a) If the spacing of maize crops was 60 cm x 91 cm, calculate the plant population on the farm:

(b) Calculate the total quantity of fertilizer used on the farm.

(c) Calculate the amount of fertilizer applied during the first two weeks of planting the maize crops.

(d) Calculate the quantity of fertilizer applied per stand at two weeks.
explanation:
(a) Iha = 10\(^{4}\)m\(^{2}\) or 10\(^{8}\)cm\(^{2}\)
plant popu./ha = \(\frac{10^{8} cm^{2}}{60 \times 91cm^{2}}\) = 18315 plants

\(\frac{10^{8} cm^{2} \times 2}{60 \times 91cm^{2}}\)

= 18,315 x 2 = 36,630 plants

(b) Total amount of fertilizer required for the farm;
Rate = 50kg / hectare
For 2 hectares = 50 x 2 = 100kg

(c) Amount of fertilizer applied at the first two weeks of planting;
Rate = 50kg / hectare
Total = 100kg for 2hectares
Required for the first two weeks = \(\frac{1}{3}\) x 100kg = 33.3kg of NPK fertilizer

(d) Quality of fertilizer per stand at two weeks
= \(\frac {\text {Quantity of fertilizer}} {\text {No of Stands}}\) = \(\frac {33.2 \times 100 {\text {grams}}} {36,630}\) = 0.9gm or 1.00gm

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
The diagram below represents an incubator. Study it carefully and use it to answer questions (a) - (e).

(a) List four components of the incubator.

(b) Explain two problems that can affect the proper functioning of any two of the components listed in (a).

(c) State three steps that should be taken to ensure that the eggs hatch at about the same time.

(d)(i) State the type of housing system adopted for breeding fowls to ensure a high rate of egg fertility.
(ii) Give reason for your answer.

(e) State two activities normally carried out by the farmer in the hatchery after hatching
explanation:
(a) Components of the incubator are:

  • heat source
  • lantern,
  • heater,
  • Electric heater.
  • Control unit
  • damper and thermostat.
  • heat distribution unit.
  • relative humidity mediation unit
  • basin of water
  • cotton wool
  • humidifier.
  • felt tray (to hold eggs and reduces cracking),
  • Egg rack,
  • Egg tray.
  • thermometer to detect degree of hotness and coldness
  • air circulation unit vents for ventilation and fan.
  • Egg turming device,
  • Insulator.

(b) Problem of functioning components: Electrical incubator:

  • Power failure
  • Iregular power supply.

For Kerosene Heat:

  • High / low voltage/ current

For Thermostat:

  • High / low voltage/ current.

For Fan:

  • Irregular power supply; Overheating caused by over-usage. Loose nuts of the parts; improper lubrication of the movable parts.

For Thermometer:

  • Cracking or breakage of the bulb/thermometer and loss of sensitivity.

For Ventilation:

  • Blockage of vents, Water Basin - Leakage, Dryíng-up of water content.

Insulator:

  • Puncturing of the insulator unit turning devices for egg tray, Lubrication of the movable joints/ parts, Loose nuts.

(C) Efforts to ensure uniformity of hatching:

  • setting eggs uniformly in the tray,
  • timely and regular turning of eggs,
  • ensure suitable environmental conditions
  • temperature, ventilation, and relative humidity.
  • proper handling of the eggs,
  • setting eggs at the same time,
  • holding period of egg before setting ( not more than 14 days).

(d) The housing type that ensures a high rate of egg fertility is Deep litter.

Reasons: mating of birds is regular and without hindrance.

(e) Activities to be carried out after hatching are-

  • sexing of chicks;
  • dryíng of chicks and intra -ocular.
  • NDV vaccination,
  • sorting out abnormal chicks and packing.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: List and explain the four major systems of land tenure in Nigeria.
explanation:
Four major systems of Land tenure in Nigeria are: (i) Communal land tenure system; (ii) Land tenure by inheritance, Leasehold system: (iv) By purchase

(1) Communal land tenure system : In this system: (i) The land belongs ) the community. (ii) Only members of the community can have access to the use of the land. (iii) Mechanization may be difficult to practise. (iv) The land cannot be used as collateral security. (v) Every member can use the land but cannot sell.

(2) Land tenure by Inheritance: In this system: (i) The land is transferred from parents to offsprings. (ii) It leads to land fragmentation. (iii) The land can be used as collateral security. (iv) Owners can develop the land property. (v) Perennial crops can be grown.

(3) Leasehold system: In this system: (i) Land is acquired for use over a stated period of time. (ii) Certain money or fee is paid for the stated period of time (iii) It ensures the use of available land. (iv) The farmer cannot use the land as collateral security. (v) He cannot develop the land beyond the lease agreement terms.

(4) By Purchase: In this system: (i) Land is totally acquired after paying certain amount of money. (ii) The land can be used as collateral security. (iii) Perennial crops can be grown. (iv) High cost prevent purchase of land by farmers. (v) Ownership may be difficult to establish

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Write short notes on the following sources of farm power:(a) Work animals; (b) Machine engines, (c) Water (d) Sun
explanation:
Short notes on: (a) Work animals as a source of farm power: (i) Work animals include bulls, horses, donkey, camels etc. (ii) These animals are used to perform certain farm operations like ploughing, ridging, etc. (iii) Some are used for transportation. (iv) Cost of maintaining the animals is high. (v) Diseases and poor handling of animals can lead to poor performance.

(b) Machines/engines (mechanical power) as a source of farm power: (i) It involves the use of modern implements like tractors, ploughs, harrow; ridger etc. (ii) It works faster and efficiently. (iii) It reduces labour cost and overall cost of production. (iv) It performs a wide range of farm operation. (v) Its capital investment is high.

(c) Water as a source of farm power: (i) It involves the use of water to generate power, e.g. Hydro-electric power, i.e, electricity. (ii) The power generated by water can be used to supply light to farms, provide heat and operate other farm equipment.

(d) Sun as a source of farm power: (i) This is the power derived from the sun ( or solar power). (ii) Energy stored from the sun can be used to power engines and tractors. (iii) The power is used by green plants for photosyn-thesis. (iv) It can be used to dry some food products. (v) Excess of power from the sun can cause evaporation or transpiration.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) What is weathering ?

(b) Enumerate three processes involved in rock weathering

(c) List and discuss briefly the agents of two of these processes
explanation:
(a) Rock weathering is defined as the process whereby rocks are broken down into tiny pieces to form the soil.

(b) Three processes involved in rock weathering are: (i) Physical weathering/process (ii) Biological weathering/process (iii) Chemical weathering/process

(c) Agents of rock wethering are;

  • Water: causes hydration of rocks and minerals.
  • Gravity: an agent of physical weathering and causes abrasion.
  • ice: in cold regions, ice formation and subsequent thawing, changes the gap size between rock fragments, loosens them and causes weathering.
  • acids: acid attack is a common cause of weathering. carbonic acid and sulfuric acid are commonly implicated as agents of chemical weathering.
  • salts: crystal formation of salts in the gaps and fissures also causes weathering of rocks.
  • winds: can shape and move loose fragments, thus progressively weathering the rocks.
  • plants: grow into crack and fissures. The expanding roots, uptake of chemicals and release of chemicals into rocks causes weathering.
  • animals: burrow animals break down rocks, other animals use other means to cause weathering.
  • temperature changes: heating and cooling enhances weathering.
  • pressure changes
  • gases: such as carbon dioxide, oxygen, sulfur oxide, etc. causes acidification or oxidation of rocks.
  • microorganisms: break down, acidify and chemically attack soil and rocks and causes weathering.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Distinguish between irrigation and drainage (b) State and explain four systems of irrigation and two of drainage.
explanation:
The difference between irrgation and drainage is that (a) irrigation is the artificial application of water to soil or land in order to supplement insufficient rainfall, while drainage is the process whereby excess water is removed from the soil or farmlands

 (b) Four systems of irrigation are: (i) Surface irrigation (ii) Sub-surface irrigation (iii) Sprinkler irrigation (iv) Drip irrigation.

Two systems of drainage are: (i) Surface drainage (ii) Sub-surface or underground drainage.

(i) Surface irrigation: (i This could be in form of flooding, furrow, contour ditch. (ii) Water flows along the surface of land to farms. (iii) Wate overflows its banks and water the surrounding areas. (iv) It is very cheap and water is distributed evenly on the so: surface. (v) Volume of water may be difficult to control especially in flooding.

(ii) Sub-surface irrigation: (i) Water applied below the ground surface. (ii) It involves the use of tiles or perforated pipes. (iii) Evaporation losses from soil is minimal. (iv) Used in soils having a low water holding capacity. (v) It does not disturb the movement of machines.

(iii) Sprinkler irrigation: (i) Water is sprayed into the air and allowed to fall on the ground in form of rain (ii) Water is pumped out through the nozzles. (iii) It requires high water pressure to operate. (iv) It is convenient in areas of wate scarcity. (v) It is very expensive to purchase and maintain.

(iv) Drip irrigation: (i) Water is discharged through nozzles called emitters. (ii) The emitters deliver water to the soil surface near the base of the plant. (iii) It economises the use of water. (iv) It operates with slower line pressure. (v) It is also expensive to set up and maintain.

(i) Surface drainage (i) It is the orderly removal of excess water from the land surface. (ii) It involves the use of open ditches, field drain and land grading. (iii) It is very cheap to construct. (iv) It occupies good land space that can be used for planting. (v) ii hinders the passage of machine like tractors.

(ii) Subsurface or underground drainage: (i) It is the orderly remove of excess water from the soil. (ii) It involves the use of tiles or moles which are buried below the ground. (iii) It does not pose any threat to machines. (iv) It leaves the field free from surface obstruction.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Describe briefly the economic importance and the control measures of the following: (a) Stem borers of cereal, (b) Grain weevils of stored produce: (c) Cotton stainer; (d) Cassava mealy bug
explanation:
The economic importance and control measures of:

(a) Stem borers of cereals: Economic importance (i) Larvae bore into stems and destroy the tissues. (ii) They make the plant to be weak. (iii) They reduce the quality an quantity of yield.

Control measures: (i) Spray with insecticides, e.g. vetox 85, to kill the larvae. (ii) Practice crop, rotation. (iii) Destroy affected plants.

(b) Grain weevils of stored produce: Economic importance: (i) Boring adult,. and larvae feed on the grains. (ii) They cause 3-5% losses and reduce farmer's profit. (iii) They reduce the grain tc powder. (iv) They reduce the quality and market value of grains.

Control measures: (i) fumigate store with BHC powder or phostoxin tablets. (ii) Ensure adequate storage. (iii) Early harvesting prevent weevils attack.

(c) Cotton stainer: Economic importance (i) It causes leaf distortion. (ii) It transmits viral disease to cotton. (iii) It destroys the content of the boll. Control measures: (i) By handpicking of insects (ii) Spray with insecticides

(d) Cassava mealybug: Economic importance: (i) It causes the disturbance of cassava growth. (ii) Plant becomes stunted deformed and twisted.

Control measures: (i) Early planting. (ii) Treat cuttings with hot water at 520c for 10 minutes

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Write short notes on the following: (a) Selective exploration; (b) Deforestation; (c) Regeneration (d) Afforestation
explanation:
(a) Selective exploration: This involves the cutting down of matured trees while allowing the younger ones to reach maturity before they are cut down.

(b) Deforestation: This is the process involved in the cutting down of trees to provide for materials needed by man. Deforestation should be carried out carefully so that the forest or land is not exposed to the agents of erosion.

(c) Regeneration: This is the ability of a plant to easily grow back after it has been cut down. Trees that have the ability to easily grow back when cut can be cut at a faster rate that those that will not easily produce new shoots after they have been cut down.

(d) Afforestation: This is the planting of new trees to replace the ones that have been cut down. This ensures the continuous availability of trees all the time. Afforestation in other words, is the growing of tree seedlings to make a forest.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss rinderpest disease of livestock under the following sub-headings: (a) Causal organism; (b) Transmission c) Symptoms (d) Effects and economic importance (e) Control
explanation:
Discussing rinderpest disease of livestock under: (a) Causal organisms : virus.

(b) Method of transmission: Rinderpest is contagious and is transmitted through contact, contaminated water, feeds an I excroment,i from infected animals.

(c) symptons: (i) High fever (ii) Difficulty in breathing (iii) Iesions on mucus membrane (iv) High mortality (v) Blood stained diarrhoea

(d) Effects and economic importance: (i) Loss of weight by animals (ii) High death rate mortality) (iii) Loss of profit by the farmer

(e) Control:(i) Regular vaccination (ii) Isolation of infected animals (iii) Restrictions of cattle movement from place to place

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) What is meant by agricultural extension ? (b) Discuss briefly how the following can be used disseminating new idea and techniques to farmers in Nigeria. (i) mass media; (ii) individual method; (iii) group method
explanation:
(a) Agricultural Extension is an informal system of education whereby information on new Innovations and ideas are disseminated to farmers

(b) The various ways to disseminate new idea sand techniques to farmers in Nigeria are:

i) Mass media: This method involves the reaching of many people with extension messages. These messages must be general so as to be of interest to all. Such mass media include posters, agricultural shows , leaflets, cinema, newspaper/magazines, radio and television

(ii) Individual method: This involves direct contact between the farmers and the extension officers Such method includes farm and home visits, office call, telephone calls, correspondence and result demonstrations

(iii) Group method: This involves the extension officers and a group of farmers it includes methods and result demonstrations, general meetings, lectures, workshops, field works and excursions.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Define farm records and farm accounts. (b) Give seven reasons for keeping such records.
explanation:
(a) Farm records are documents of all the activities pertaining to the farm business while farm accounts are statements of money spent and received for goods and services used in a farming business

(b) Seven reasons for keeping farm records are:

(i) For checking on costs and returns.

(ii) For comparing management efficiency over time.

(iii) it provides farm planning data to guide future management decisions.

(iv) For easy procurement of loans from banks

(v) For the purpose of proper tax assessment of the farm

(vi) It provides a history of what has happened on the farm for the period under consideration.

(vii) To know whether profit or loss is made

(viii) To keep a close check on whether of not things are going according to plan on the farm

subject: Agricultural Science
q: a) Describe five factors required for establishing a fish pond (b) Enumerate six management practices for maintain-rig high fish yield
explanation:
(a) Five factors required for establishing a fish pond are: (1) Adequate water supply: (i) Water is one of the most important factors necessary for siting a fish pond because the habitat of fish is water. (ii) The water must be clean and of high quality. (iii) The right quantity must be available (iv) The water must be free from pollution. (v) The water must not have bad smell, taste or colour.

(2) Soil in the area: (i) Fertile soil is the best soil because it contains nutrients (ii) Clay soil must be prevented in the area because of its ability to hold water

(3) Topography (or shape of the land): The land must be slopy to (i) ensure easy drainage of water during harvesting. (ii) enable water to flow into the pond

(4) Vegetation of the area: (i) Low vegetation, e q. grassland is preferred (ii) Woodlands tiro not suitable because it will increase the cost of the project through clearing and stumping.

(5) Availability of supplementary feed stuffs: (i) This promotes rapid growth of fish. (ii) It permits high stocking density of fish. (iii) It ensures early harvesting of the fish.

(b) Six management practices for maintaining high fish yield are. (i) Regular feeding (ii) Removal of weeds from pond (iii) Regular supply of clear and clean water (iv) Prevention of fish diseases (v) Keeping off predators, (vi) Regular harvesting to prevent over population

subject: Agricultural Science
q:

(b) State three factors responsible for alkalinity in the soil.

(c) Mention three factors responsible for acidity in the soil

(e) List five liming materials.
explanation:
(a) The pH status as indicated by the pointer reads 5.0 which indicates that the soil is slightly acidic.

(b) Factors responsible for the alkalinity in the soil are:

  • concentration of soluble salts in the soil
  • Lack of rainfall
  • use of acid-fertilizers.

(c) Factors responsible for the acidity in the soil are:

  • leaching
  • use of acid fertilizers
  • presence of acid parent materials
  • Nutrient uptake by plants
  • presence of sulphur in the soil

(d) Alkalinity in the soil can be removed by:

  • use of acid fertilizers
  • application of sulphur to the soil  
  • irrigation.

(e) Liming materias are; 

  • limestone
  • slaked lime
  • Quicklime
  • wood ash
  • Calcium hydrogen carbonate.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss eight problems of agricultural development in Nigeria and suggest one possible solution to each of the problems
explanation:
The problems of agricultural development in Nigeria and one possible solution each are

(i) Inadequate land: The type of land tenure system, increasing population in Nigeria and deforestation do not make land available for agriculture. Solution: The government should implement the land use decree of 1978

(ii) Poor storage and processing facilities: These facilities are inadequate, costly, scarce and these eventually reduce the quality and quantity of agricultural produce. Solution: Government should provide storage and processing facilities at subsidised rate.

(iii) Lack of finance: Farmers do not have access to loan. They lack collateral security and the interest rate is very high. These prevent development of agriculture. Solution: Interest rate charged by banks should be reduced so that farmers can borrow money.

(iv) Inadequate farm input: Farm inputs are also scarce, inadequate, expensive and most farmers lack the technical know - how to operate them hence low agricultural procutivity. Solution: Government should reduce the cost of of farm inputs.

(v) Poor transportation Network; Most farms are not easily accessible; roads are bad coupled with lack of vehicles and high cost of transportation and these hinder agricultural development. Solution: Government Should construct good roads in rural areas

(vi) Lack of basic amenities: Social amenities like electricity, pipe borne water, health care services etc are lacking in rural areas and these affect agricultural production. Solution: Government through DFRRI should provide these amenities in rural areas.

(vii) Inadequate agricultural education and extension services: Most farmers are illiterate, conservative and crude. Extension officers are inadequate and inefficient hence, low agricultural productivity. Solution: Mass literacy campaign should be launched where farmers can be trained in modern method of farming.

(viii) Poor marketing system: There is no organised marketing system for farm produce. Produce are sold immediately after harvesting. Solution: Government agencies should buy produce from farmers and store for future consumption.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: a) i) What Is farm surveying ? (ii) Outline the importance of farm surveying. (b) List and discuss four survey equipment commonly used in the farm.
explanation:
(a)(i) Farm surveying is a series of activities, mainly measurements, performed in the field to locate the farm land, delineate its boundaries, determine its size and produce a map of the farmland.

(ii) Importance of farm surveying e: include: (i) it helps to know the size of the farm. (ii) It helps in the production of the soil map of the farm. (iii) It ensures good planning of the farmstead. (iv) It demarcates the farm boundaries and identifies its features and the location.

(b) Four surveying equipment commonly used in the farm are: (i) Ranging pole (ii) Gunter's chain (iii) Measuring tape (iv) al Prismatic compass.

(i) Ranging poles: These are wood or metal poles used in marking spots or stations during surveying.

(ii) Gunter's chain: This is a surveyor's chain of 100 links. 22 meters long used to measure a piece of land.

(iii) Measuring tapes: These are used in taking short or detailed measurements

(iv) Prismatic compass: This is used to measure horizontal angles between a reference median and a point of interest in order to find the orientation of the point and its bearing.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Mention two major elements of climate. (b) Discuss six ways by which climate and vegetation influence soil formation
explanation:
(a) Two major elements of climate are: (i) Rainfall (ii) Temperature.

(b) Six ways by which climate and vegetation influence soil formation are: (i) Rainfall through its running water helps to weather rocks. (ii) The chemical action of water also helps to break rock to form soil (iii) Expansion and contraction of rocks due to high temperature helps to break-down rocks. (iv) The roots of trees in the forest helps to break down rocks to form soil. (v) Wind carrying rock particles breaks other rocks to form soil (vi) Fallen leaves from trees decay to form humus in the soil.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Explain the term soil structure and state its importance in agriculture. (b) State four properties each of clay and sandy soil.
explanation:
(a) Soil structure: This refers to the ways in which the different particles of the soil are packed or arranged. lt also refers to the shape and arrangement of primary particles to form compound. Examples are crumb, plate-like,prismatic and spheroidal structures.

The Importance of soil structure include: It determines the (i) level of aeration of the soil. (ii) the fertility of the soil. (iii) the amount of living organisms in the soil

(b) (i) Four properties of sandy soil are: (i) It is coarse and large - grained. (ii) It is loose and gritty. (iii) It has large air pores hence. it supports aeration. (iv) It cannot form. ribbon or castes since it is not sticky when wet.

(b)(ii) Four properties of clay soil are: (i) clay soil is smooth and finely grained (ii) It has small airpores. (iii) It is sticky, when wet. (iv) It supports erosion and water logging.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) State the laws of Mendel (b) Discuss three advantages and two disadvantages of: (i) introduction-, (ii) selection in crop improvement
explanation:
(a) The laws of Mendel are in two parts: (i) Mendel's first law of segregation of genes states that genes are responsible for the development of the individual and that they are independently transmitted from one generation to another without undergoing any alteration.

(ii) Mendel's second law of independent assortment of genes states that each character behaves as a separate unit and is inherited independently of any other character.

(b)(i) Three advantages of introduction in crop improvement are (i) it helps to introduce new varieties of crops to the new area. (ii) It may enhance greater productivity. (iii) It may perform better if conducive climatic conditions are present in the new area.

(ii) Two disadvantages of introduction in crop improvement are (i) It may introduce new diseases to the new area. (ii) The new plants may not adapt to the new area. (iii)

Three advantages of selection in crop improvement are: (i) It reduces the spread of .ests and diseases. (ii) It ensures that only the best naturally available crop is grown. (iii) Crops with desired qualitities are selected .

While two of the disadvantages of selection in crop improvement are: (i) It is tedious and time (ii) requires expertise which may not be easily available.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) What is rangeland ? (b) Discuss seven practices of management and improvement of rangeland
explanation:
(a) Rangeland refers to a piece of land containing natural grasses, legumes and herbs which can be eaten by tam animals. In other words, it is a natural pasture,

(b) Seven practices of management and improvement of rangeland are: (i) Burning to encourage regrowth of grasses. (ii) Fencing to ensure rotational grazing. (iii) Weeding to speed up the growth  of pastures. (iv) Fertilization to increase yield of pasture. (v) Pest and disease control to ensure rapid growth of Pasture. (vi) Irrigation to ensure continuous growth of pasture especially during the dry season (vii) Adequate stocking to prevent overgrazing

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Explain how each of the following practices can bring about high yield from a fish pond: (a) deweeding: (b) desilting (c) supplementary feeding (d) fertilization.
explanation:
Practices which can bring about high yields from a fish pond include:

(a) Deweeding: This is the removal of weedy pond. It allows the dissolution of oxygen in water and the penetration of sunlight to the bottom of pond which aids the growth of fish food (planktons). It also prevents the built up of pests and diseases.

(b) Desilting: This is the remove of l silts from water to make the water to be clean. It promotes easy movement of fish within the water and prevent Pollution of water.

(c) Supplementary feeding (i) It ensures the availability of balance feed for the fish and ensure'. rapid growth of fish. It promotes multiplication of fish through reproduction.

(d) Fertilization: It ensures the availability of nutrients for the growth of planktons and increases the rapid growth of fish

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Enumerate eight functions of an extension agent in the life of a rural farmer
explanation:
Eight functions of an extension agent in the life of a rural farmer are:

(i) Agricultural extension teaches improved practices to farmers in the cultivation, rearing and processing of crops and livestock.

(ii) It acts as an intermediary between farmers and research institutes.

(iii) It encourages farmers to increase productivity of food, cash crops and livestock.

(iv) It helps to collect and collate basic information relating to rural economy which are used for implementing certain programmes.

(v) It educates rural women in home management (vi) It helps to identify proper marketing channel to sell their products.

(vii) Agricultural extension helps to raise the standard of living of farmers. (viii) It helps to change people's attitude towards their problems.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Explain the meaning of agricultural finance and agricultural credit. (b) Name six sources of agricultural credit available to the small-scale farmer (c) Mention six problems associated with agricultural credits.
explanation:
Agricultural finance is the act of acquisition and use of capital in agriculture. It deals with the supply and demand for funds in the agricultural sector of the economy while agricultural credit is a repayable loan given to farmers with or without interest to enable them carry out their farm operation more efficiently.

(b) Sources of agricultural credit available to the small-scale farmer are: (i) Agricultural Banks like Nigerian Agricultural and Cooperative Bank (N.A.C.B.). (ii) Supervised Agricultural credit schemes. (iii) Thrift and saving societies. (iv) Self financing or personal savings.(v) Money lender. (iv) Cooperatives societies. (c) Six problems associated with Agricultural credits are: (i) High interest rate. (ii) Lack of collateral security. (iii) Diversion of loan by .borrowers. (iv) Long gestation periods, especially for plantation crops (v) High incidence of loan defaulters. (vi) Difficulties in recovering loan

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) State four roles of government in agricultural production (b) Describe four traditional land ownership system in Nigeria and give one disadvantage of each system.
explanation:
(a) Four roles of government in agricultural production are:

(i) Provision of financial assistance in form of loans and credit facilities to farmers

(ii) Establishment of agricultural programmes such as Farm Settlement Scheme, Green Revolution, Operation feed the Nation, etc.

(iii) Provision of farm inputs such as fertilizers, farm tools and implements, seeds, agro chemicals etc.

(iv) Provision of basic amenities like pipe borne water, electricity and hospital in rural areas.

(b) four traditional systems of land ownership in Nigeria and their disadvantages are :

(i) Communal and Tenure System: The land belongs to the community and only members of the family can have may not have access to the use of such land. Disadvantages: (1) Mechanization may be difficult. (2) Non-members of the community may not have access to the use of the land.

(ii) Land tenure by Inheritance: This is a situation where land is inherited from one's parents. Disadvantage: It leads to land fragmentation

(iii) Lease hold system: This requires the payment of a fee or tenancy for land used over a stated period of time. Disadvantage: The farmer cannot use the land s as collateral security.

(iv) Purchase: This is the total acquisition of land for a fee. Disadvantage: Ownership may be challenged or difficult to establish

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss eight maintenance practices including the precautions you would take to keep the tractor operating safely and ensure its durability.
explanation:
Eight maintenance practices and precautions which can be taken to keep a tractor operating safely and ensure durability after use are:-

(i) Inspect the tractor before starting everyday. It helps to spot potential machine failures and safety hazards.  

(ii)Ensure that nuts and screws or metal parts are tight. Loose nuts, screws and metal parts may cause vibration and produce irritating noise and in some cases may cause machine failure.

(iii) Keep the machine clean by removing all  thrash or mud and excess grease/oil from machine at the start or at the end of operation.

(iv) Do not overload the tractor i.e. operate at appropriate speed for field operations and avoid using it under conditions that are unfavourable.

(v) Employ a skilled or competent and experienced tractor operator.

(vi) Carry out routine services by changing filters and oil when due.

(vii) Check water and oil levels regularly to ensure that they are normal and to prevent any overheating or seizure of engine.

(viii) Check tyre pressures and replace flat or worn-out tyres.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) With the aid of a fully labelled diagram, describe the carbon cycle. (b) Explain the importance of carbon cycle to Crops and animals
explanation:
Diagram of a carbon Cycle is shown below

(b) Importance of carbon cycle to crops and animals are:

(i) Carbon in the atmosphere is taken up by plants for the production of starch through photosynthesis.

(ii) Plants give out oxygen while animals give out carbon dioxide during the process of respiration thus maintaining the atmospheric level of carbon dioxide and oxygen.

(iii) The released carbon provides the major building block of all organic matter.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Describe briefly the side effects of preventive and control measures of method diseases and pests of crops under the following headings: (a) chemical control (b) biological control (c) cultural of control .
explanation:
The side effects of various preventive and control measures of diseases and pests of crops are:

(a) Chemical method of control:

(i) Almost all the chemicals used are toxic to man and domestic animals.

(ii) Some beneficial insects of soil organisms may be destroyed.

(iii) Pests and diseases may develop resistance to chemical. (iv) Some chemicals may cause water pollution.

(v) Empty containers could be a source of poison when used as containers for consumables.

(b) Biological method of control:

(i) The new organisms introduced may start attacking crops which were formerly free from attack.

(ii) The predators introduced may feed on other beneficial insects causing imbalance in the ecosystem.

(c) Cultural method of control:

(i) The use of fire may spread to other farms

(ii) The use of fire may kill some beneficial organisms.

(iii) It can lead to destruction and losses of organic matter in the soil.

(iv) It can also destroy the soil structure

subject: Agricultural Science
q: a) Describe briefly five management methods adopted in the forest area to ensure a continuous timber supply. (b) Enumerate six benefits derived from forestry and range
explanation:
(a) Five management methods adopted in the forest areas to ensure a continuous timber supply are:

(i) Forest Regulations : These are laws made by the government to conserve or preserve forest trees. No person can cut down timber without government permission.

(ii) Selective exploration: Forest trees can be cut down while allowing others to reach maturity before they too are cut

(iii) Afforestation: This is the planting of new trees to replace the ones that have been cut.

(iv) Fire prevention: This prevents annual bush burning and where possible, establishes fire breaks.

(v) Taungya system : This system allows the farmer to plant his crops after which they will plant economic trees on the same piece of land.

(b) Six benefits derived from forestry and range are:

(i) Supply of food such as fruits, seeds. bush meat, etc.

(ii) Supply of fuel (fire wood). (iii) Provision of timber for furniture and buildings.

(iv) Provision of employment to some people. (v) Forest is a source of medicinal herbs.

(vi) Provision of pulpwood for paper making.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Explain briefly the following terms: (a). ovulation; (b) oestrus cycle; (c) gestation (d) parturition
explanation:
(a) Ovulation : This is the rupturing of the ovary wall to release mature eggs (ova) or ovum into the oviduct of the female animal. It is under the control of the pituitary gland and it occurs in between oestrus cycle.

(b) Oestrus cycle : This is the interval from the end of one heat period to the beginning of another. It is the period of heat in farm animals and it is controlled by a hormone. It varies from one animal to another, e.g. Cow = 20 - 21 days; Ewe = 7 -21 days; Sow --- 14 - 28 days .

(c) Gestation:This is the time between conception and birth, during which the embryo or fetus is developing in the uterus

(d) Parturition : This is the act of giving birth in farm animals. It marks the end of pregnancy and the beginning of lactation. The act of giving birth in Cow - Calving, Sow - Farrowing, Ewe - Lambing, Doe (Goat) Kidding.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: a) What is balanced ration ? (b) If birds at point of lay were fed a ration/feed containing: maize meal - 65°/ , groundnut cake - 25%, fish meal - 5% and brewers' grain - 5%. Name the other feed ingredients that are essential which are not listed. State the importance of the ingredients you have listed.
explanation:
(a) Balance ration is the feed containing all essential nutrients in the correct amount and adequate proportion for feeding animals.

(b) Essential ingredients not included are: (i) Oyster shell or bone meal (ii) Fats and oil (iii) Mineral salts (iv) Vitamin or vitamin supplement.

(c) Importance of the ingredients listed above include:

(i) Oyster shell or bone meal:

(1) Farm animals need calcium and phosphorus for bone and teeth development.

(2) Birds need calcium and phosphorus for egg shell formation.

(3) It also supplies other essential minerals like sodium, copper, etc for osmotic balance

(ii) Fats and oil:

(1) It provides carotene rich in vitamin A.

(2) It provides an additional source of energy

(3) It is required for good egg yolk formation.

(4) It prevents dustiness in feed.

(iii) Mineral salt:

(1) Salt improves palatability of feed.

(2) It supplies sodium as sodium chloride.

(3) It is useful for enzymatic functions in intestinal tract.

(iv) Vitamins or vitamin supplements:

(1) They provide the necessary vitamin requirements.

(2) They are necessary for good egg formation.

(3) They are necessary for good bone formation.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Explain agricultural marketing and four channels of marketing farm produce. (b) State four problems associated With marketing of farm produce by small-scale farmers.
explanation:
(a)(i) Agricultural marketing involves ail the activities re-quired to move farm produce from the producers (farmers) to the final consumers.

(ii) Channels of marketing farm produce are:

(1) Local markets where sellers and buyers are in close contact.

(2) Cooperative Societies which facilitate the flow of bulk sales and enhance the bargaining power of farmers.

(3) Commissioned agents who buy directly from the farmers in bulk and sell to marketing boards and exporters.

(4) Exporters who sell the commodities to overseas buyers (consumers).

(b) Four problems associated with marketing of farm produce by small scale farmers are:

(i) Inadequate stor-age facilities

(ii) Produce are perishable.

(iii) Transportation is grossly inadequate.

(iv) Poor prices of products.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: List six environmental factors affecting agricultural production in Nigeria. (b) Explain briefly how five of the factors listed in (a) can affect agricultural production in Nigeria
explanation:
(a) Six environmental factors affecting agricultural production in Nigeria are (i) Rainfall (ii) Temperature (iii) Wind (iv) Parasites (v) Pests and diseases (vi) Light/sunshine

(b) Agricultural production in Nigeria can be affected by:

(a) Rainfall (1) It is necessary for seed germination. (2) It ensures availability of nutrient to plants by dissolving it. (3) It affects the distribution of plants and animals. (4) Excessive rainfall may cause soil erosion.

(b) Wind: (1) It increases the rate of transpiration in crops. (2) High wind velocity may cause wind erosion. (3) It aids seed dispersal. (4) It aids pollination of flowers.

(c) Light/sunshine: (1) It is necessary for photosynthesis. (2) It affects evapo-transpiration in crops. (3) Some crops exhibit photoperiodism

(d) Parasites: (1) They transmit diseases. (2) They may kill host animals and plants. (3) They reduce the quantity and quality of crops or livestock.

(e) Pests and diseases: (1) They reduce the quality and quantity of crops and livestock. (2) They decrease the farmers' profit. (3) They transmit diseases from one crop to another.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: State the law of demand and six basic assumptions as related to agricultural products. Illustrate your answer with the aid of a demand schedule and a demand curve
explanation:
(a) The law of demand states that the lower the price, the more or higher the quantity of agricultural produce that will be demanded or the higher the price, the lower the quantity of produce that will be demanded.

(b) Six basic assumptions as related to agricultural products are that:

(i) No very close substitutes for commodity exist.

(ii) There is no change in tastes and preference of the consumers.

(iii) The amount of money or income of the consumers remain constant.

(iv) There is no change in the quality of products.

(v) The habits of consumers remain unchanged.

(vi) The prices of other goods remain constant.

Example of a Demand schedule

Price (=N=) Quantiy demand (kg)
80 10
60 20
40 30
20 40

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
Study the diagrams below

 

     

 

(a) ldentify the implements represented by diagrams l and ll above.

(b) List three important parts of each of the implements illustrated by diagrams I and lI

(c) State one function of each of the parts of the implements listed in (b)

(d) State:(i) one major advantage in using the implement illustrated by Il:
(ii) one major disadvantage in using the implement illustrated by I.
explanation:
(a) Implement I is Mould board plough/ plow while implement lI is Disc plough /plow.

(b) Important parts of each are:

  • I - Coultier /vertical disc; shore / share point mould board: beam/frame; standard.
  • II - Concave disc / disc: sledge / knife / scrapper, rotating hubs disk bearing bearing: spring: beam / frame, balancing disc / furrow wheel; beam cap; coupling point / hinge / linkage point, Standard

(c) Function of each part listed; 

I;

  • Coulter makes vertical cuts/furrows;
  • Share makes horizontal cuts/uproot weeds;
  • Mould board carries the soil, inverts the weeds and exposes the roots of the weeds;
  • Standard bears the mouldboard and Share
  • Beam/Frame carries the plough and other components / proves additional weight for greater depth

II;

  • Concave disc rotates and makes vertical cuts / horizontal cuts as the tractor moves forward / inverts the soil.
  • Scrapper /knife/sledge helps to remove soils that cling to the disc.
  • Hub helps the dick to overcome obstacles like rocks. outcrops, stumps etc;  
  • Frame / Bame holds discs in position and bears additional weight for greater depth
  • Furrow wheel /balancing disc - balances the plough / keeps plough from toppling:
  • Coupling point provides point of attachment to the tractor;
  • Standard carries the disc and other associated parts.

(d) Advantages of Disc plough (ii) are:

  • It overcomes obstacles like stones and stumps more readily.
  • lt can be used in more difficult soils, e.g. clay or sticky soils.

Disadvantages of Mould board plough are

  • It loses its share point when in contact with an obstruction.
  • It predisposes soil to erosion / soil compaction takes place when used often.

(a) Parts labeled A - F are;

  • A - Floating organic materials;
  • B -Water;
  • C - clay / clay particles;
  • D - Silt,
  • E - fine sand / sand
  • F - Coarse sand / gravel / small stones

(b) Major constituents of soil are;

  • Minerals - sand, clay etc;
  • Organic matter;
  • Water
  • Air;
  • Micro organisms.

(c) Other methods of determining soil texture are;

  • Feelíng method by rubbing soil between thumb and the fingers
  • Moulding into rings when wet.
  • Sieve method. 
  • Pipette method.

(d) Importance of soil texture to the farmer:

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
(a) Identify the fruit illustrated by the diagram below

(b) Name the parts labelled A to D.

(c) Outline briefly the processes or stages involved in the cultivation of the fruít illustrated in the diagram.
explanation:
(a) The fruit is Coconut fruit / Oil palm fruit.

(b) Parts labelled A - D are:

  • Á -Epicarp / Exocarp;
  • B- Mesocarp / husk;
  • C- Endocarp/shell;
  • D - Kernel / coconut kernel / Endosperm /seed:

(c)(i) Stages of cultivation / Steps of production for Coconut fruit are:

  • Plant coconut fruits in nursery at a spacing of 30 -60 cm x 30 -60cm,
  • water and weed regularly,
  • prepare the land through cleaning, burning, and stumping.
  • Dig planting holes infield at 8m x 8m spacing and transplant the seedlings.
  • Add fertilizer/ manure for good growth, weed, water, and control pests and disease when necessary. OR

(ii) Stages of cultivation for oil palm fruit are;

  • pre-germination of kernels in seedbox and planting pre-germinated seedlings in the nursery.
  • Water and weed regularly as required.
  • Land preparation, clearing, burning, stumping.
  • Digging holes at 9m x 9m spacing.
  • Transplanting of seedlings.
  • Add manure / fertilizer for good growth.
  • weed when necessary.
  • water when necessary.
  • control pests and diseases when necessary

(d) Industrial uses of Coconut or oil palm fruit.

Coconut fruit;

  • husk used in manufacture of foot mats / brushes.
  • cake for livestock feeding.
  • shell Used for fuel.
  • copra used in manufacture of coconut oil.
  • oil used in making creams/ soap/ margarine.
  • coconut milk used for alcohol production.
  • Biscuits.

Oil palm fruit;

  • Husk used for fuel.
  • Oil used for soup/margarine / cosmetics.
  • Shell used for fuel.
  • cake used for livestock feed.
  • oil used for candle production.
  • kernel used for production of palm kernel oil.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss briefly four factors that cause food shortages in Nigeria
explanation:
Four factors that cause food shortages in Nigeria are:

(a) Poor storage and preservation facilities: (i) Most agricultural products are perishable. (ii) Lots of farm products are lost because of lack of storage facilities. (iii) Most produce are wasted due to poor storage. (iv) Technical know-how on food storage is inadequate or lacking.

(b) Poor transportation network: (i) Presence of bad roads prevent evacuation of produce to market and this leads to wastage

(ii) The number of vehicles are inadequate. (iii) There is high cost of transportation. (iv) Most farms are not linked by roads and this eventually leads to wastage of produce.

(c) Lack of finance or credit facilities: (i) Peasant farmers have no access to credit facilities to expand their farm-land, (ii) High interest rate charged by banks prevents borrowing by farmers. (iii) Farmers do not have the required collateral security to enable them secure loan for expansion. (iv) Poor finance results in the purchase of inferior farm inputs which result in food shortage.

(d) Poor harvesting and processing techniques: (i) Premature harvesting results in food shortages. (ii) Late harvesting also results in food shortages. (iii) Bad method of harvesting also causes food shortages. (iv) Lack of modern methods of food processing also causes food shortages.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss briefly eight advantages of agricultural mechanization in Nigeria
explanation:
Eight advantages of agricultural mechanisation in Nigeria include the following:

(i)Agricultural mechanization enhances timeliness of farm operations.

ii) It saves time.

(iii) Agricultural mechanization increases productivity.

(iv) It reduces health hazards.

(v) It promotes specialization of available manpower.

(vi) It encourages cooperation among farmers.

(vii) It also increases farm revenue.

(viii) It promotes agro-allied industrial establishment

subject: Agricultural Science
q: List and discuss the process of soil formation
explanation:
The process of soil formation include:

(1) PHYSICAL PROCESS: (i) The grinding of rock surfaces by solid materials carried by wind. water and moving ice results in the breaking of the rocks. (ii) The freezing and melting of ice within the rock results in the breakage of rocks (iii) The continuous expansion and contraction of rocks due to variations in the intensity of temperature results in the breaking down of rocks. (iv) The action of running water also eats up the rocks. resulting in breaking down of rocks;

(2) BIOLOGICAL PROCESS: (I) The actions of some animals like earthworm and termite cause rock to break. (ii) The burrowing activities of earthworm and termite into rocks cause their breakage. (iii) Roots of plants which penetrate rocks also help to break down the rocks. (iv) Human activities during tillage, use of plough and harrow also indirectly break down rocks.

(3) CHEMICAL PROCESS (i) Solution: Water dissolves soluble minerals or rocks in the soil. (ii) Oxidation: The reaction of oxygen with rocks results in breakage of rocks. Carbonation: Carbon dioxide which dissolves in water to form a weak carbonic acid reacts with rock to break it down. (iv) Hydration : The attachment of water to rocks results in their breakage.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) (i) Define the term irrigation (ii) Mention two types of irrigation (b) Discuss briefly six problems association with irrigation.
explanation:
(a)(i) Irrigation is the artificial application of water to soil or land in order to supplement short fall in rain. (ii) Two types of irrigation are: (i) Surface irrigation e g. flooding, furrow, contour ditch. (ii) Overhead irrigation e.g. Sprinkler and Drip

(b) Six problems associated with irrigation are:(i) Inadequate water source from streams, rivers and lakes. (ii) Irrigation equipment are expensive to purchase (iii) Pests from dry surroundings may invade and damage crops on irrigated lands. (iv) Lack of technical know-how on the operations of irrigation. (v) Vectors of diseases like snails, tse-tse fly breeds freely in irrigated areas. (vi) Disease-causing organisms increase with humidity due to irrigation.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss eight economic importance of insect pests in crop production
explanation:
Eight economic importance of insect pests in crop production include the following:

(i) Insect pests reduce the quality of crops either in the field or in the store.

(ii) They destroy crops in the field through their biting chewing, sucking and burrowing activities.

(iii) They reduce the yield and may even lead to total crop failure. (iv) Farmers profits are drastically reduced.

(v) Cost of production is increased as large sum of money is expended on their control

(vi) There is also reduction in viability of stored produce.

(vii) They are disease carrying agents (or vectors).

(viii) They render vegetables and fruits unattractive and unmarketable.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss the production of yam under the following headings : (a) materials of propagation; (b) soil requirement; (c) land preparation; (d) planting and spacing; (e) cultural practices (f) three major pests and their control.
explanation:
Production of yam: (A) Materials of propagation : Yam seeds, yarn setts and mini-setts are used for propagation

(B) Soil requirement: Yarn requires (i) loamy soil rich in humus (ii) sandy loam soil (iii) well drained soil

(C) Land preparation This includes (i) the clearing of the land or vegetation (ii) Ploughing and harrowing (iii) Making of ridges )r mounds or heaps. (D) Planting and spacing: (i) Early planting is done at the onset of dry season (Oct - Nov) (ii) Late planting is done at the beginning of rainy season (Feb - March) (iii) Yarn seeds/setts are placed in the soil with cut surface up but slanted (iv) Spacing is 90 cm x 100 cm

(E) Cultural practices : These include: (i) The application of appropriate fertilizer. (ii) Regular weeding to ensure the growth of crop. (iii) Mulching to prevent evaporation and Insure water conservation. (iv) Staking to spread out leaves to receive sunlight (v) Training of vines.

(F) Three major pests and their control are: (i) Yam beetle Control : By dusting seed yam or yam sett with insecticides eg Aldrin dust before planting (ii) Termites Control : (i) Destroy the termitorium or ant hill (ii) Spray with insecticides (iii) cricket : Spray with insecticides

subject: Agricultural Science
q: (a) Name five breeds each of cattle and pig that can be found in Nigeria (b) State six general characteristics rabbits
explanation:
(a) Five breeds of cattle that can be found in Nigeria are:

i) White Fulani

(ii) Sokoto Gudali

(iii) Muturu

(iv) Ndama .

(v) Kuri

(b) Five breeds of pig that can be found in Nigeria are:

(i) Large white

(ii) Large black

(iii) Duroc Jersey

(iv) American Land race

(v) Yorkshire

(c) Six characteristics of rabbits are:

(i) Rabbits possess a short gestation period (31 days).

(ii) They have a small body size.

(iii) They are very prolific with large litter size.

(iv) They have good quality meat which is even more palatable than chicken.

(v) They grow very fast and reach maturity weight in about 5 - 6 months.

(vi) They are efficient converter of wide range of vegetable matter.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: a) Name the causative agent of each of the following livestock diseases: (i) food and mouth; (ii) rinderpest; Newcastle (b) Name three animals that can be affected by each of the diseases listed in (a). (c) State fo symptoms of foot and mouth disease and three symptoms of each for rinderpest and Newcastle diseases
explanation:
(a) The causative agent of: (i) Foot and mouth disease is virus (ii) Rinderpest disease is virus (iii) Newcastle disease is virus

(b) Three animals that can be affected by each of the diseases in (a) above are: (i) Foot and Mouth disease - Cattle, Sheep and Goat. (ii) Rinderpest disease - Cattle, Pig, Sheep. (iii) Newcastle disease - Domestic fowl, Turkey, Duck.

(c) Four symptoms of foot and mouth disease are: (i) Formation of blister on the mucous membrane of the mouth (ii) Lameness (iii) Inflammation of the teats and udder (iv) Lesion in the interdigital spaces of the hoofs.

Three symptoms of Rinderpest disease are: (i) High fever (ii) Difficult breathing and loss of weight (iii) Loss of appetite and high mortality. Three symptoms of Newcastle disease are: (i) Nervous disorder or twisting of the neck (ii) Presence of greenish diarrhoea (iii) High mortality and low egg production.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss eight problems which an agricultural extension worker can face in the discharge of his duties.
explanation:
Eight problems which an agricultural extension worker can face in the discharge of his duties are:

(i) Language barrier leads to improper dissemination of new innovations.

(ii) Inadequacy of well trained extension workers makes him have a wider area and more farmers than he can cope with.

(iii) Inadequate resources such as finance and materials tend to hinder his performance.

(iv) High level of illiteracy among farmers may slow down the rate of adoption of new innovations.

(v) Inadequate publicity - mass media are not often fully utilised in publicising extension programmes.

(vi) Poor transportation or lack of it retards the performance of the extension worker.

(vii) Insufficient motivation of extension worker by way of remuneration makes him reluctant to work.

(viii) Unfavourable attitude of rural farmers toward government programmes, makes adoption of new innovations difficult.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: List and discuss four functions of a farm manager in a farm business.
explanation:
Four functions of a farm manager

(a) Organisation: The farm manager (1) secures suitable land for farming. (ii) determines what to produce. (iii) . determines s the scale of production. (iv) procures credit or loan for farming.

(b)  Administration: The farm manager (1) supervises the work on the farm. (ii) arranges work rosters. (iii) directs staff on day to day activities.(iv) Makes arrangement for staff welfare

(c) Production: The farm manager (i) is responsible for purchase and use of farm inputs. (ii) ensures the adequate supply of feeds. (iii) makes use of modern farming  techniques. (iv) ensures the health of animals/crops on the farm.

(d) Marketing: The farm manager (i) determines what to sell (ii) determines how much to sell (iii) determines when to sell to make huge profits (iv) is responsible for processing of produce.

subject: Agricultural Science
q:
Study carefully the experimental set-ups below and use them to answe the following questions;

(a) What is the aim of the experiment for which the apparatus has been set-up?

(b) List the experimental set-ups in the order which as risen through the soil samples, startin with the highest.

(C) List six items of apparatus used in the experimental set-up

(d) Describe briefly the procedure used in setting up the experiment. 

(e) Why is there a difference in the set. in the water levels of the soil  samples in the set ups labelled B and C
explanation:
(a) The aim of the experimental set up is to compare / deterrnine and measure the capillarity of rise in water of diferent types of soils.

(b) The rise in water is in the following order:

  • Specimen A- highest- loam,
  • Specimen B - higner - Clay
  • Śpecimen C-lowest- sand

(c) Apparatus used in the experimental set up are:

  • a large beaker / basin/dish/trough.
  • 3 capillary tubes/ glass tubes 
  • Soil samples A, B and C.
  • Water
  • Cotton wool
  • clamps/ retort stands

(d) Method used:

  • Take three glass tubes and plug each with cotton wool.
  • Fill each tube with dry samples of clayey, loamy and sandy soils respectively.
  • Fix the three tubes in an upright position with clamps
  • Get the three glass tubes immersed in a trough of water,
  • Allow to stand for the required time.
  • Watch the upward movement into the soil samples and record your observations,

(e) The difference in level of water in the B and C is because sample B has very fine pores which allow water to move upwards because of strong capillarity attraction while in sample C; there are large pores with low capillary attraction hence, upward moverment of water is Slow.

subject: Agricultural Science
q: Discuss briefly the ways in which the following factors contribute to the problems of agricultural development in Nigeria: (a) finance; (b) farm input; (c) transportation; (d) storage and processing facilities.
explanation:
The following factors contribute to the problems of agricultural development in Nigeria:

(a) Finance (i) Peasant farmers have no access to credit facilities or loan to increase their production. (ii) Commercia banks will not give credit/loan to farmers because they do not have collateral security. (iii) Farmers need adequate finance and credit to embark on large scale farming. (iv) Banks charge high interest rate. (v) Some farmers who get loan divert the money for other purposes.

(b) Farm inputs: (1) Majority of the farm inputs are not produced in Nigeria, but have to be imported. (ii) Farm inputs are inadequate or are in short supply. (iii) Most of the farm inputs are of inferior quality. (iv) Farm inputs even when they are available are not supplied at the right time. (v) Most farmers do not know how to use or apply some of the inputs.

(c) Transportation: (i) Due to bad roads, much of the produce spoilt and wasted, especially perishable ones. (ii) Some existing farms are located in inaccessible areas where roads are completely absent. (iii) Cost of transportation is very high. (iv) There are inadequate vehicles. (v) Poor transport system makes evacuation of farm produce to urban areas difficult.

(d) Storage and processing facilities: (i) Excess produce are wasted due to inadequate processing. (ii) Processing/storage facilities are very expensive. (iii) Inadequate storage facilities result in wastage and loss of some agricultural products. (iv) Improper storage reduces the quantity and quality of some of the products.(v) Lack of technical knowledge on operation of Processing and storage facilities
